did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for the classification of seven types of skin diseases (including cancerous and non-cancerous conditions) using the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involves CNN layers (conv2d, maxpool2d, batch normalization, dense) and a Softmax classifier to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin disease classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section detailing the architecture (conv2d, maxpool2d, etc.), a results section with specific accuracy metrics (Table II), and a conclusion summarizing the new findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAITPR), it reports original technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Recognition of Type of Skin Disease Using CNN
journal: 2022 First International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Trends and Pattern Recognition (ICAITPR)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to classify seven types of skin diseases, including four cancerous and three non-cancerous conditions, using the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involves preprocessing skin images to remove noise, followed by feature extraction and classification via CNN layers such as conv2d, maxpool2d, and batch normalization, ending with a Softmax classifier. To address dataset imbalance, the authors ordered the data by adding duplications, which contributed to achieving high accuracy. The model was evaluated on specific disease categories, yielding detection rates ranging from 89% for Basal Cell Carcinoma to 97% for Actinic Keratoses and Melanoma. The results demonstrate that the proposed system provides a fast, accurate, and cost-effective alternative to traditional diagnostic methods for dermatological diseases.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as cancerous; detection rate 97% (33/35 images).

Classified as non-cancerous; detection rate 92% (33/35 images).
Classified as non-cancerous; detection rate 90% (83/92 images).

Classified as non-cancerous; detection rate 91% (90/99 images).

paper_terms: Pyogenic granulomas and haemorrhage(Lead to cancerous)
lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as cancerous (or leading to cancerous); detection rate 94% (66/70 images).

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Pyogenic Granuloma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin diseases using a CNN with a Softmax classifier. The model predicts one of seven discrete diagnostic categories (four cancerous, three non-cancerous) from the HAM10000 dataset. Output is a discrete class label, not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopy images used for the classification of seven types of skin diseases. The authors noted the dataset was unordered and required ordering by adding duplications to address imbalance and achieve high accuracy.
classes_used: Pyogenic granulomas and haemorrhage (Lead to cancerous)

use.training: Used for training the CNN model; the dataset was ordered by adding duplications to handle imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing the system's detection rate on 365 total images across seven disease categories.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are presented as detection rates and accuracy percentages.

HAM10000.notes: The dataset was unordered and made ordered by adding duplications to address imbalance. The total number of test images was 365. The system successfully detected diseases across all seven categories with detection rates ranging from 89% to 97%.

architecture_details.notes: The architecture includes convolutional layers with 2D kernels, max pooling for dimensionality reduction, batch normalization for standardization, and dense layers for classification. The final layer uses a Softmax classifier for probabilistic distribution across seven classes.

classification_or_output_head.description: The output of the neural network is classified using a probability distribution via the SoftMax layer to identify the exact disease among seven types.

preprocessing_techniques: Filtering and processing to remove unwanted noise and enhance the image
Dataset ordering by adding duplications to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The HAM10000 dataset was originally unordered. The authors addressed class imbalance by adding duplications to the dataset to make it ordered, which contributed to achieving high accuracy. No specific geometric or color transforms, GANs, or test-time augmentations were reported.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) system successfully detects and classifies seven types of skin diseases (four cancerous and three non-cancerous) with high accuracy.
The system provides a fast, accurate, and cost-effective alternative to traditional diagnostic methods, potentially reducing the high costs and time associated with skin disease diagnosis.
The model achieved detection rates ranging from 89% for Basal Cell Carcinoma to 97% for Actinic Keratoses and Melanoma on the HAM10000 dataset.
Addressing dataset imbalance by ordering the HAM10000 dataset through duplication contributed to achieving high accuracy.
The system can serve as a reliable real-time teaching tool for medical students in dermatology.
The technology can correlate with dermatologist results using modern computational algorithms, thereby enhancing performance standards in health studies and aiding in better diagnosis.
The study acknowledges that future work could involve adding more classification types to the system.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, non-representative test subset of the HAM10000 dataset (365 images total), which limits the generalizability of the reported high accuracy (89-97%).

The authors address class imbalance by 'ordering' the dataset via duplication rather than using standard data augmentation or resampling techniques, which risks data leakage and overestimation of model performance.

Melanoma is incorrectly classified as 'Non-cancerous' in the paper's terminology and Table II, despite being a malignant cancer, indicating a significant labeling or classification error in the study's framework.
The paper lacks rigorous statistical validation; no p-values, confidence intervals, or hypothesis testing are reported, and results are presented solely as detection rates on a single split.
The methodology section lacks critical training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, learning rate, number of epochs, and batch size, making the study difficult to reproduce.

The input image resolution is not explicitly stated, though layer output shapes suggest an input size of approximately 64x64 pixels, which may be insufficient for detailed dermoscopic feature extraction.

The paper is published in a conference proceeding (ICAITPR) with limited peer review scope compared to high-impact journals, and the claims of 'high accuracy' are based on a potentially biased dataset handling strategy.
